questions.q: Why does the Buddha call the going-forth a "noble search" (ariyapariyesana)?
questions.a: He distinguishes it from the ignoble search — seeking what is subject to birth, aging, sickness, and death in things that are also subject to those. The noble search is to seek, while being subject to those things oneself, what is not subject to them: the unconditioned, nibbāna. Same searcher, different object.

questions.q: The Bodhisatta trained with two teachers before his awakening. Who were they, and what happened?
questions.a: Āḷāra Kālāma, who taught him the base of nothingness; and Uddaka Rāmaputta, who taught him the base of neither-perception-nor-non-perception. In both cases the Bodhisatta quickly mastered what the teacher taught. Both teachers offered him joint leadership of their communities. He refused — because he could see these attainments were still conditioned, did not lead to disenchantment or cessation, and were not yet liberation.

questions.q: The act of walking away from two highly respected teachers is canonical. What is the lesson?
questions.a: Reverence for a teacher does not require staying with their teaching past the point where it has taken you as far as it can. The lesson is not ingratitude — the Bodhisatta names both teachers respectfully. The lesson is that the path outranks any particular lineage, and a serious practitioner must eventually be willing to leave even their best teachers when the teaching has finished its work for them.

questions.q: After his awakening, the Buddha initially wondered whether to teach at all. Why?
questions.a: He reflected that the dhamma is subtle, profound, peaceful, going against the current of conventional thinking; and that most beings are attached to sensuality, consumed by lust and hatred. It would be wearying to teach to people who could not hear. He only decided to teach after Brahmā Sahampati appealed to him to look again — and the Buddha saw that there are some, like lotuses rising above the water, who would hear.

questions.q: Whom did the Buddha first consider teaching, and why could he not?
questions.a: Āḷāra Kālāma and Uddaka Rāmaputta, out of gratitude for what they had given him. A devatā informed him that both had recently died. The sutta notes this with no drama — simply that he saw with the eye of wisdom where each had been reborn, and that it was a loss.

questions.q: The Buddha first taught the five former companions who had practiced asceticism with him. What were their names, and why did they matter?
questions.a: Kondañña, Bhaddiya, Vappa, Mahānāma, Assaji. They had abandoned him when he took food and gave up extreme asceticism. When he approached them awake, they initially resolved not to greet him — and then found they could not resist, because his presence was no longer that of a renunciate in struggle. This is the first sangha. MN 26 is the sutta from which the vow text of this app is drawn.
